heading: Modifying Propositions
body: Logic utilizes variables to represent propositions, a concept introduced in BASICS_02. A proposition can be modified to state that its original claim is false. This process is called **negation**, the act of reversing the truth value of a statement. If a sentence asserts that something is the case, its negation asserts that it is not the case.

heading: The Negation Operator
body: The symbol used for negation is the tilde (~). This is a **unary operator**, an operator that acts on only one proposition at a time. The tilde is placed immediately before the proposition variable it modifies. If $P$ represents a statement, then $\sim P$ represents the negation of that statement.
note: In some textbooks, the symbol ¬ is used instead of the tilde.

heading: Translating Negation
body: The negation of a sentence is usually formed by adding the word "not" or the phrase "it is not the case that."

examples.text: P: The sky is blue. ~P: The sky is not blue.
P: The sky is blue. ~P: It is not the case that the sky is blue.
P: The sky is blue. ~P: The sky is green.

note: A negation must specifically deny the original claim, not just offer a different claim.

heading: Truth Value Inversion
body: Negation inverts the truth value of a proposition. If the original proposition is true, its negation must be false. If the original proposition is false, its negation must be true. A proposition and its negation can never share the same truth value.

examples.text: If P is True, then ~P is False.
If P is False, then ~P is True.

heading: Negation vs. Antonyms
body: Negation is not the same as finding an opposite or an antonym. The negation of "The car is fast" is "The car is not fast," not "The car is slow." A car that is stationary is "not fast," but it is also "not slow." Always use the word "not" to ensure the logic remains precise.

heading: Key Takeaway
body: The negation operator (~) is a unary operator that inverts the truth value of a single proposition. Applying a tilde turns a true statement false and a false statement true.
